nativeRange.habitat: Swampy lowland wetlands, swamp forests, marshes, irrigation ponds, rice paddies, and still to slow-moving freshwater bodies in tropical and subtropical regions.

decorPreferences: fine gravel or soil substrate when planted

summary: A pan-tropical aquatic fern with finely-divided lacy leaves; generally considered a beginner-friendly fast grower that can be planted in the background or left floating for a lush feathery surface canopy.
careNotes: Ceratopteris thalictroides is a fast-growing pan-tropical aquatic fern that is generally considered one of the classic beginner plants for the freshwater aquarium. It can be grown two ways: planted in fine gravel, sand, or soil as a midground-to-background specimen (where it tends to develop deeper-cut, more finely-divided fern-like leaves), or left floating at the surface (where it produces a lusher, broader, more feathery canopy with trailing roots underneath that are heavily used by shrimp and fry for cover). The two growth forms look so different that floating and planted specimens are sometimes mistaken for different species. Lighting is best in the medium range — sources mostly agree on medium-to-bright light for compact growth; in low light the plant becomes leggy and sparse. CO2 is not required and has been reported as optional across all sources; supplementation accelerates an already-fast growth rate. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral, with reported tolerances spanning roughly 20–28 °C, pH 5.5–7.5, gH 3–12, and kH 3–8 across the sources consulted. As a heavy water-column feeder it strips nitrate and phosphate quickly, which makes it useful for stabilising new tanks; liquid fertilisation is generally advised in lean tanks. When planted, bury only the very base of the rhizome — burying the crown will rot it. Older leaves develop adventitious plantlets along their margins that drop off and root on their own, which is the practical propagation route in aquaria. Taxonomy note: historically placed in Parkeriaceae, now classified in Pteridaceae. Identification caveat: the genus has been repeatedly revised and C. thalictroides is sometimes lumped with C. cornuta in trade material; the closely-related 'Lace Leaf' or 'Triangle' form (C. siliquosa, sometimes treated as a synonym or subspecies) is also commonly sold under the Water Sprite name.
breedingNotes: Propagated vegetatively by adventitious plantlets that form on the margins of older leaves; the plantlets develop their own roots while still attached and detach on their own when ready to be relocated or float to a new spot. Lateral shoots from the base can also be separated. Spore reproduction occurs in emersed/fertile fronds but is impractical to manage in the submerged aquarium setting.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source for light demand (medium, 0.5 W/L), CO2 (low requirement), growth rate (fast), height (15–30 cm), placement (midground/background), care level (medium), origin (pan-tropical), and family (Pteridaceae). Located via Tropica search from the supplied URL.

additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (21–28 °C / 70–82 °F), lighting (moderate), CO2 not required, growth rate (fast), placement (background or floating), substrate (not critical, strong water-column feeder), and care difficulty (easy).
